These findings demonstrate that lipid deposition serves as a critical driver of EndoMT in SCMECs (Fig. S5A–C, Supporting Information). Notably, while ACE preconditioning failed to modulate EndoMT progression, NCE treatment exerted a potent inhibitory effect on mesenchymal transition, evidenced by significant attenuation of α-SMA and Collagen I immunofluorescence intensity (Fig. 2Q, R, S). Complementary Western blot analyses further substantiated these observations, revealing marked suppression of EndoMT-associated proteins (α-SMA) and collagenous matrix components (Collagen I/III) in NCE-treated cultures (Figs. S8A, B, C, D, Supporting Information).

Taken together, NCE exerts excellent lipid metabolism regulatory capacity, enhances the lipid droplet autophagy level in SCMECs, promotes lipid degradation, improves lipid overload, and alleviates the occurrence of endothelial-mesenchymal transition (EndoMT). This provides cytological experimental evidence for NCE in treating microvascular endothelial function and vascular homeostasis after SCI.

sec.title: Preparation and characterization of “Microenvironment-Reprogramming Potent Hydrogel”
sec.p: GelNB is a novel hydrogel featuring superior porous architecture, mechanical performance, and tissue adhesiveness. Recent studies have harnessed it for bone and cartilage regeneration [26], while we are the first to pioneer its application in the treatment of SCI. To comprehensively delineate the disparities between GelNB and GelMA, their microstructures, mechanical characteristics, and adhesive functionalities were subjected to rigorous characterization. As illustrated in Fig. 3A, both GelMA and GelNB hydrogels exhibited a liquid-like state before UV irradiation, while they displayed rapid gelation capability after UV exposure. Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) micrographs revealed distinct porous morphologies: 10% GelNB formed a three-dimensional porous network with uniform pore size and high interconnectivity (average pore diameter: (45 ± 5) μm), whereas 10% GelMA exhibited heterogeneous pore distribution and poor connectivity (average pore diameter: (28 ± 4) μm) (Fig. 3B). This morphological divergence was further validated by quantitative porosity analysis (Fig. 3C) - GelNB achieved a porosity of (85.2 ± 3.1) %, which was significantly higher than that of GelMA ((62.5 ± 4.3) %). Such superior porosity and interconnectivity not only furnish ample channels for nutrient transport and metabolic waste efflux but also construct a biomimetic microenvironment conducive to cell colonization and proliferation, serving as the foundational structural prerequisite for advanced biomedical hydrogels.

For mechanical performance, stress-strain curves (Fig. 3D) demonstrated that GelNB exhibited a tensile strength of (1.82 ± 0.15) MPa and a fracture strain of (320 ± 25) %, representing 91.6% and 52.4% enhancements compared to GelMA (tensile strength: (0.95 ± 0.11) MPa; fracture strain: (210 ± 18) %), respectively. Cyclic tensile tests (Fig. 3E and F) revealed that after 100 repeated tensile-relaxation cycles, GelNB retained (89.3 ± 2.7) % of its initial stress and achieved a strain recovery rate of (85.6 ± 3.1) %, whereas GelMA only maintained (72.1 ± 3.5) % stress retention and (68.4 ± 2.9) % strain recovery. These findings confirm GelNB's superior mechanical robustness and fatigue resistance. Stress relaxation assays (Fig. 3G) further demonstrated that GelNB had a stress relaxation time constant τ of (182 ± 12) s, significantly longer than that of GelMA ((105 ± 8) s), indicating its enhanced capacity to buffer dynamic mechanical loads in physiological environments and mitigate mechanical damage to adjacent tissues.

For viscoelastic properties, comparative analyses showed that at 1 Hz frequency, GelNB's storage modulus (G′) was (2850 ± 120) Pa (1.7-fold higher than GelMA's (1680 ± 95) Pa), and its gelation time was (45 ± 3) s (42.3% shorter than GelMA's (78 ± 5) s). Separate dynamic rheological characterizations exclusively for GelNB (Fig. 3H–J) further delineated its advantageous viscoelastic behavior: Fig. 3H (dynamic frequency sweep) revealed minimal G″ fluctuation (12.5%) across 1-100 Hz, reflecting robust frequency-independent structural stability; Fig. 3I (dynamic time sweep) confirmed rapid gelation kinetics; Fig. 3J (dynamic amplitude sweep) demonstrated strong resistance to deformation under varying oscillatory amplitudes. Collectively, these data validate GelNB's structural stability under dynamic physiological conditions, aligning with the mechanical demands of native tissues.

In terms of adhesive capacity, multi-modal test results consistently confirmed the significant advantages of GelNB: in lap shear strength tests (Fig. 3K–M), the bonding strength of GelNB to porcine skin tissue reached (45.6 ± 3.2) kPa (2.05-fold that of GelMA ((22.3 ± 2.8) kPa)); tensile adhesion tests (Fig. 3N–P) showed that GelNB's maximum peeling force was (1.25 ± 0.10) N (83.8% higher than GelMA); in T-peel tests (Fig. 3Q–S), GelNB's peel strength was (18.7 ± 1.5) N, significantly higher than GelMA's ((9.2 ± 1.1) N). This exceptional adhesive performance originates from multiple intermolecular interactions (e.g., hydrogen bonding, hydrophobic interactions, and covalent bonding) between specific functional groups in GelNB and tissue surfaces, enabling the formation of a stable tissue-gel interface in humid physiological environments and effectively preventing post-implantation displacement or detachment.

For biological safety evaluation, both in vitro and in vivo assessments were performed. First, cells were seeded in GelMA and GelNB hydrogels. Live/dead staining demonstrated that cells maintained excellent viability in both hydrogels (Fig. S11A and B, Supporting Information). Furthermore, sEVs were mixed into equal volumes of the two hydrogels at equal mass. After gelation, the hydrogels were placed in 24-well plates and incubated with 1 mL PBS to monitor the protein concentration in the supernatant. Comparative analysis showed that GelNB exhibited a slower release rate of sEVs compared with GelMA, suggesting that GelNB provides a better sustained-release effect in vitro (Fig. S12A, Supporting Information). For the in vivo study, the degradation rates of the two hydrogels were compared. Briefly, 10 μL of each hydrogel was implanted into the spinal cord injury region, and the residual weight was recorded at different time points. At day 28, more residual GelNB was detected than GelMA, and the overall degradation rate of GelNB was slower (Fig. S12B, Supporting Information). In addition, H&E staining showed no obvious histological damage or toxicity in the liver, kidney, or spleen after treatment with either hydrogel (Fig. S13, Supporting Information), indicating that both hydrogels possess favorable biocompatibility and are suitable for in vivo animal studies.

In summary, compared with the classic GelMA hydrogel, GelNB has achieved leapfrog advancements in core performance metrics - porous microstructure (higher porosity, superior interconnectivity), mechanical robustness (enhanced tensile strength, recoverability, and stability), and tissue adhesiveness (doubled strength under multi-modal tests) - via synergistic structural and performance optimization. Its comprehensive properties closely match the practical requirements of biomedical applications, offering a more clinically translatable hydrogel platform for fields such as neural tissue regeneration and wound repair.

sec.title: “Microenvironment-Reprogramming Potent Hydrogel” significantly promotes neural function recovery after SCI
sec.p: Given the complex microenvironment of the injured area and the persistent presence of myelin debris following SCI, continuous therapy is particularly crucial. To mimic the natural concentration of sEVs in the circulating plasma of neonatal mice, we innovatively mixed purified sEVs (NCE and ACE) with 10% GelNB, and adjusted the total protein concentration to 2.0 μg per microliter of GelNB. Eventually, an injectable and highly adhesive composite hydrogel - “Microenvironment-Reprogramming Potent Hydrogel” - was successfully fabricated (Fig. 3T).

Subsequently, we investigated and compared the differences in the ability of NCE and ACE to repair SCI. First, we evaluated the loading effect of GelNB with sEVs. Under a confocal microscope, Dil dye-labeled sEVs were observed to be uniformly distributed within it (Fig. 4B), indicating that GelNB has good loading capacity. In in vivo experiments, equal volumes (10 μl) of NCE@GelNB and ACE@GelNB were administered in a local dressing manner and fixed by light cross-linking (Fig. 4A). The results of in vivo imaging of small animals showed that the retention time of DiR dye-labeled ACE and NCE in the injured area of mice was extended to more than 14 days after injury (Fig. 4C and D), and sEVs could achieve a sustained-release effect in the injured area.Fig. 4
Therapeutic effect of the “Microenvironment-Reprogramming Potent Hydrogel” promotes neurological function recovery after SCI. A) Schematic diagram of the “Microenvironment-Reprogramming Potent Hydrogel” treatment for SCI in mice. B) Immunofluorescence images showing the spatial distribution of Dil-ACE and Dil-NCE (red) in GelNB. C, D) In vivo fluorescence imaging of the injured area at different time points post-SCI in mice with DiR-labeled ACE and NCE, along with quantitative analysis (n = 3). E, F) Basso Mouse Scale (BMS) scores and BMS subscores in mice of different treatment groups following SCI (n = 6). G-I) Detection of motor evoked potentials (MEPs) and quantitative statistical analysis of amplitude and latency (n = 6). J-O) Analysis of hindlimb locomotor function in the Control, ACE, and NCE groups after SCI, based on reconstructed hindlimb movement trajectories. Quantitative parameters (n = 6) including maximal iliac crest height (J, K), stride length (L, M), and maximal toe height (N, O) were measured to assess motor recovery. P, Q) Representative immunofluorescence micrographs of TUBB3 (red) and DAPI (blue) in each group, along with quantification results (n = 6). R, S) Representative images of AAV2/9-SYN-ZsGreen-labeled cortical projections (white) in each group (n = 6). Data are presented as mean ± s.d. Statistical analysis was performed using one-way analysis of variance (ANOVA) for comparisons. ∗, p < 0.05; ∗∗, p < 0.01; ∗∗∗, p < 0.001; ∗∗∗∗, p < 0.0001; ns, no significance.

Continuous assessments using the Basso Mouse Scale (BMS) and its subscale scores over a 28-day recovery period demonstrated that mice in the NCE treatment group consistently outperformed the control group and the ACE group from post-injury day 14 onward (Fig. 4E and F), thereby confirming the therapeutic value of NCE intervention. Motor Evoked Potentials (MEPs) can detect pathological changes in neural conduction along the entire corticospinal tract. At post-injury day 28, mice treated with NCE@GelNB exhibited significantly shortened latency and markedly enhanced amplitude responses (Fig. 4G, H, I), further indicating that NCE plays a positive role in repairing neuromuscular integrity following SCI. To quantitatively evaluate functional recovery, kinematic analysis of hindlimb movement trajectories was performed at the 28-day endpoint. The NCE@GelNB treatment group achieved partial but statistically significant motor function recovery, with significant improvements observed particularly in three key biomechanical indicators: maximum iliac crest elevation (Fig. 4J and K), stride length (Fig. 4L and M), and maximum toe extension (Fig. 4N and O). Collectively, these therapeutic outcomes from a multi-dimensional evaluation integrating standardized behavioral metrics, quantitative kinematic assessments, and neurophysiological validation fully demonstrate that “Microenvironment-Reprogramming Potent Hydrogel” can significantly improve functional recovery after SCI, with remarkable efficacy especially in enhancing motor coordination and neural conduction efficiency.

Immunofluorescent interrogation of TUBB3 revealed markedly attenuated neural fiber density with restricted spatial distribution in the perilesional zone of control specimens. Strikingly, NCE administration elicited a robust augmentation in both the numerical abundance and topographical dispersion of TUBB3+ neural fibers in the immediate vicinity of the lesion core (Fig. 4P and Q). Complementary anterograde tracing employing AAV2/9-SYN-ZsGreen-labeled cortical projections demonstrated definitive termination at the lesion boundary under control conditions, whereas NCE treatment facilitated remarkable axonal elongation into the distal territory beyond the injury site (Fig. 4R and S), with therapeutic efficacy demonstrably surpassing both control and ACE-treated cohorts. TEM results demonstrated that, compared to the Control group and ACE-treated group, NCE treatment ultimately promoted axonal regeneration and myelin remodeling (Figs. S14A, B, C, D Supporting Information).

Collectively, our multi-dimensional research findings demonstrate that the “Microenvironment-Reprogramming Potent Hydrogel” therapy effectively enriches regeneration- and repair-associated factors derived from 2-day-old neonates, thereby exhibiting substantial therapeutic potential for improving neurological function following SCI. These observations further imply the existence of unique bioactive constituents in NCE.

sec.title: “Microenvironment-Reprogramming Potent Hydrogel” reduces lipid deposition in SCMECs and inhibits the progression of EndoMT after SCI
sec.p: Concomitant with the perturbation of lipid homeostasis in SCMECs post-SCI, the aberrant vascular architecture within the lesion epicenter predominantly comprises microvascular endothelial cells undergoing endothelial-mesenchymal transition. These pathological vascular structures exhibit significant luminal dilation and compromised endothelial tight junction integrity, predisposing to hematogenous cellular extravasation and proteinaceous diapedesis [38,39].

In our therapeutic intervention, we implemented site-specific administration of age-stratified sEVs at the SCI locus. Temporal analysis at the 2-week post-injury milestone revealed that NCE treatment elicited a marked constriction of neovascular caliber-a cardinal indicator of vascular functional integrity-while leaving the vascular morphology in uninjured spinal regions unaltered (Fig. 5A and B).Fig. 5
Treatment with the “Microenvironment-Reprogramming Potent Hydrogel” remodels vascular structure and reprograms the lipid metabolic function of SCMECs. A) Immunofluorescence micrographs of CD31+ microvessels (green) in the SCI core area and uninjured area under different treatment conditions 2 weeks post-treatment. B) Quantitative analysis of microvascular diameter in the SCI core area and uninjured area (n = 6). C, D) Accumulation of PLIN2+ lipid droplets (green) in SCMECs and quantitative analysis under different treatment conditions 2 weeks post-treatment (n = 6). E-H) Representative immunofluorescence micrographs and quantitative analysis of CD31+ SCMECs undergoing EndoMT (α-SMA, Collagen I, Fibronectin) (red) under different treatment conditions 4 weeks post-treatment (n = 6). Data are presented as mean ± s.d. Statistical analysis was performed using one-way analysis of variance (ANOVA) for comparisons. ∗, p < 0.05; ∗∗, p < 0.01; ∗∗∗, p < 0.001; ∗∗∗∗, p < 0.0001; ns, no significance.

Prior investigations have established that SCMECs orchestrate the phagocytic clearance of myelin debris through IgG-mediated opsonization, wherein the autophagy-lysosomal cascade constitutes the principal degradation pathway [8]. By post-injury day 14, three-dimensional confocal imaging (x-z and y-z orthogonal planes) unequivocally demonstrated engulfment of LDs within SCMECs, as evidenced by perilipin 2 (PLIN2) immunostaining-the hallmark lipid droplet-associated protein (Fig. 5C and D). Quantitative analysis uncovered a pronounced augmentation of LDs deposition within microvascular endothelial compartments of control and ACE-treated cohorts at the 14-day post-injury timepoint. Strikingly, NCE intervention elicited a significant attenuation of LDs accumulation within SCMECs, while leaving the foundational phagocytic process of myelin debris intact. These in vivo findings collectively indicate that NCE therapy selectively disrupts the pathological conversion of myelin debris into LDs within the neurovascular unit, without compromising the essential scavenging function of microvessels.

In vitro validation confirmed prior observations that myelin debris exposure induces endothelial phenotypic switching. Notably, while ACE preconditioning failed to modulate EndoMT progression, NCE treatment exerted a potent inhibitory effect on mesenchymal transition, evidenced by significant attenuation of α-SMA and Collagen I immunofluorescence intensity (Fig. 5E, F, G, H).

In essence, these multidimensional investigations establish NCE as a potent modulator of post-SCI vascular remodeling, capable of simultaneously targeting lipid metabolic dysregulation and EndoMT progression.

sec.title: NCE-loaded miR-487b-3p abrogates EndoMT and modulates lipid clearance in SCMECs
sec.p:
Comprehensive miRNA sequencing analysis identified a unique cohort of miRNAs exhibiting significant enrichment in NCE relative to ACE (Fig. 6A, Fig. S9, Supporting Information), with miR-665-3p, miR-380-3p, miR-487b-3p, miR-377-3p, miR-135a-5p, miR-708-5p, miR-127-5p, miR-376a-3p, miR-136-3p and miR-136-5p demonstrating the most pronounced and stable upregulation profile. Subsequent RT-qPCR validation of the top ten differentially expressed miRNAs corroborated the remarkable enrichment of miR-487b-3p in NCE (Fig. 6B), providing compelling evidence for its potential intercellular trafficking via sEVs and subsequent cellular internalization across diverse tissues and organs. These findings collectively posited miR-487b-3p as a pivotal regulatory node mediating NCE's capacity to restore lipid metabolic equilibrium in SCMECs and facilitate SCI repair.Fig. 6
miR-487b-3p in NCE regulates lipid metabolism and inhibits EndoMT in SCMECs. A) Clustering heatmap of miRNA sequencing data from ACE and NCE. B) RT-qPCR quantitative detection of the top 10 miRNAs ranked by P-value <0.05 and log2FC > 1 from the miRNA sequencing results (n = 3). C) RT-qPCR quantitative detection of the transfection efficiency of miR-487b-3p mimic and inhibitor (n = 3). D, E) Representative immunofluorescence micrographs showing EndoMT (α-SMA, red) in SCMECs (CD31, green) after transfection with miR-487b-3p mimic/inhibitor, and quantitative analysis of α-SMA fluorescence intensity (n = 6). F) Western blot analysis of PLIN2, LAMP2, and PPARα protein expression in SCMECs treated with PBS/Mimic/Mimic negative control (NC) and myelin debris under different conditions. G-I) Quantitative analysis of PLIN2, LAMP2, and PPARα protein expression levels (n = 3). J) Western blot analysis of PLIN2, LAMP2, and PPARα protein expression in SCMECs treated with PBS/NCE + Inhibitor/NCE + Inhibitor NC and myelin debris under different conditions. K-M) Quantitative analysis of PLIN2, LAMP2, and PPARα protein expression levels (n = 3). N-P) Representative immunofluorescence micrographs of lipid droplet markers PLIN2 (red) and BODIPY (green) in SCMECs treated with PBS/Mimic/Mimic NC and myelin debris under different conditions, along with quantitative analysis of PLIN2 and BODIPY fluorescence intensity (n = 3). Q-S) Representative immunofluorescence micrographs of lipid droplet markers PLIN2 (red) and BODIPY (green) in SCMECs treated with PBS/NCE + Inhibitor/NCE + Inhibitor NC and myelin debris under different conditions, along with quantitative analysis of PLIN2 and BODIPY fluorescence intensity (n = 3). Data are presented as mean ± s.d. Statistical analysis was performed using one-way analysis of variance (ANOVA) for comparisons. ∗, p < 0.05; ∗∗, p < 0.01; ∗∗∗, p < 0.001; ∗∗∗∗, p < 0.0001; ns, no significance.

To mechanistically interrogate this hypothesis, we engineered miR-487b-3p mimics encapsulated within exosomes, achieving robust transfection efficiency in vitro (Fig. 6C). Upon NCE intervention in SCMECs, we observed a marked upregulation of miR-487b-3p expression (Fig. S16, Supporting Information). Phenotypic analysis of SCMECs revealed that while myelin debris exposure alone induced conspicuous endothelial-mesenchymal transition characteristics, cells treated with miR-487b-3p mimic-loaded liposomes exhibited marked attenuation of EndoMT pathology following identical myelin debris challenge (Fig. 6D and E).

We further elucidated miR-487b-3p′s regulatory role in lipid homeostasis by comprehensive profiling. Quantitative Western blot analysis demonstrated that miR-487b-3p transfection significantly diminished PLIN2 protein accumulation in myelin debris-stimulated SCMECs (Fig. 6F and G), with orthogonal immunofluorescence microscopy confirming concomitant reductions in both PLIN2+ and BODIPY+ LDs formation (Fig. 6N–P). Concurrently, assessment of lysosomal acidification demonstrated that miR-487b-3p effectively restored lysosomal activity in SCMECs damaged by myelin debris (Fig. S7A and B, Supporting Information). Notably, this miRNA-mediated modulation restored the expression of critical regulators of lipid catabolism-LAMP2 and PPARα-suggesting preserved functionality of both lysosomal degradation pathways and mitochondrial fatty acid β-oxidation.

To elucidate the specificity of miR-487b-3p′s functional impact, we developed an inhibitor targeting miR-487b-3p using chemically modified antisense oligonucleotides (ASOs) with a targeted antagonism strategy. Co-transfection experiments revealed that while NCE treatment alone significantly upregulated LAMP2 and PPARα expression while attenuating lipid deposition, concurrent miR-487b-3p inhibitor completely abrogated these protective effects (Fig. 6J–M, and 6Q-S). These findings definitively establish miR-487b-3p, when encapsulated within neonatal plasma-derived sEVs, as an essential molecular determinant governing lipid metabolic homeostasis regulation.

sec.title: miR-487b-3p selectively suppresses myelin debris-elicited hyperactivation of the IRS1-PI3K-Akt axis in vascular endothelial cells, Thus facilitating lipid catabolism
sec.p:
miRNAs execute their biological functions through precise complementary base pairing with specific recognition elements within target mRNAs, thereby inducing either translational repression or mRNA degradation-mediated gene silencing [40,41]. By employing an integrative bioinformatics approach utilizing multiple high-confidence public target prediction databases (TargetScan, PITA, miRmap, and miRanda), we systematically identified a cohort of potential target genes including Irs1, Astn1, Nsmf, Glcci1, Gm45062, and Cxcl12. Among these candidates, Irs1 and Cxcl2 emerged as particularly compelling targets warranting further investigation based on their predicted functional relevance (Fig. 7A).Fig. 7
miR-487b-3p targets and inhibits the overactivated IRS1-PI3K-Akt signaling pathway induced by myelin debris, thereby reprogramming lipid metabolism and attenuating EndoMT in SCMECs. A) Venn diagram of downstream target genes predicted by PITA, miRmap, miRanda, and TargetScan. B) RT-qPCR quantitative detection of Irs1 mRNA levels after transfection with miR-487b-3p mimic/inhibitor (n = 3). C) RT-qPCR quantitative detection of Cxcl12 mRNA levels after transfection with miR-487b-3p mimic/inhibitor (n = 3). D, E) Western blot analysis and quantitative analysis of IRS1 protein expression in SCMECs after transfection with miR-487b-3p mimic/inhibitor (n = 3). F, G) Western blot analysis and quantitative analysis of CXCL12 protein expression in SCMECs after transfection with miR-487b-3p mimic/inhibitor (n = 3). H) Binding sites of mmu-miR-487b-3p with the 3′ untranslated regions (3′UTRs) of Irs1 mRNA and Cxcl12 mRNA, as well as the construction of binding site mutants—Irs1-3′UTR-mut and Cxcl12-3′UTR-mut. I) Dual-luciferase reporter assay and quantitative analysis of the interaction between mmu-miR-487b-3p and target genes Irs1 and Cxcl12 (n = 3). J, K) Western blot analysis and quantitative analysis of IRS1 protein expression in SCMECs at different time points after myelin debris treatment (n = 3). L-P) Western blot analysis of IRS1, PI3K, p-PI3K, Akt, p-Akt, mTOR, and p-mTOR protein expression in SCMECs treated with myelin debris followed by PBS/Mimic/Mimic + IRS1OE intervention; quantitative analysis of IRS1 protein levels and the ratios of p-PI3K/PI3K, p-Akt/Akt, and p-mTOR/mTOR protein levels (n = 3). Q-T) Western blot analysis of PLIN2, LAMP2, and PPARα protein expression in SCMECs treated with myelin debris followed by PBS/Mimic/Mimic + IRSOE intervention; quantitative analysis of PLIN2, LAMP2, and PPARα protein levels (n = 3). U) Representative immunofluorescence micrographs of lipid metabolism-related proteins PLIN2 (red) and BODIPY (green) in SCMECs treated with myelin debris followed by PBS/Mimic/Mimic + IRSOE intervention, along with quantitative analysis of fluorescence intensity (n = 3). V) Representative immunofluorescence micrographs of mesenchymal marker α-SMA (red) in SCMECs treated with myelin debris followed by PBS/Mimic/Mimic + IRSOE intervention, along with quantitative analysis of fluorescence intensity (n = 3). Data are presented as mean ± s.d. Statistical analysis was performed using one-way analysis of variance (ANOVA) for comparisons. ∗, p < 0.05; ∗∗, p < 0.01; ∗∗∗, p < 0.001; ∗∗∗∗, p < 0.0001; ns, no significance.

We experimentally validated the regulatory effects of miR-487b-3p on Irs1 expression through a combination of RT-qPCR and Western blot analyses. Consistent with our hypothesis, transfection with chemically synthesized miR-487b-3p mimics resulted in a statistically significant downregulation of both Irs1 transcript levels (Fig. 7B) and IRS1 protein abundance (Fig. 7D and E). To definitively establish the direct interaction between miR-487b-3p and its predicted binding site within the Irs1 3′-UTR, we engineered a site-directed mutant construct (Irs1-3′-UTR-mut) and performed quantitative dual-luciferase reporter assays. The preserved luciferase activity in the mutant construct compared to the wild-type control (Irs1-3′-UTR-wt) following miR-487b-3p transfection (Fig. 7H and I) provided compelling molecular evidence for the functional significance of this specific binding site, thereby confirming their direct targeting relationship with high specificity.

IRS1 (insulin receptor substrate 1) serves as an essential molecular adaptor in insulin signaling cascades and functions as a critical regulatory switch governing both PI3K-Akt and Ras/MAPK signaling pathways that orchestrate diverse cellular behaviors [42,43]. Our previous investigations demonstrated that early phagocytosis of myelin debris by SCMECs induces significant activation of the PI3K-Akt pathway, which exhibits a positive correlation with enhanced lipid biosynthesis and pathological deposition. Temporal protein expression analysis revealed a more than 2-fold increase in IRS1 protein levels by day 3 of myelin debris exposure (Fig. 7J and K), indicating dynamic regulation during this process.

To mechanistically investigate whether NCE-derived miR-487b-3p can effectively modulate the IRS1-PI3K-Akt-mTOR signaling axis and thereby attenuate lipid droplet formation derived from phagocytosed myelin debris, we constructed an IRS1 overexpression plasmid to further delineate its potential key regulatory role. Quantitative Western blot analysis demonstrated significant reduction in phosphorylation levels of p-PI3K/PI3K, p-Akt/Akt, and p-mTOR/mTOR in SCMECs transfected with miR-487b-3p mimic (Fig. 7L–P), indicating effective suppression of this prosurvival signaling cascade through miR-487b-3p-mediated targeting of IRS1. This inhibitory effect was completely reversed by co-transfection with an IRS1 overexpression plasmid, thereby establishing IRS1's pivotal role as an indispensable upstream regulator of this signaling network.

More importantly, our comprehensive analysis revealed that miR-487b-3p mimic transfection significantly downregulated PLIN2 expression in SCMECs treated with myelin debris (Fig. 7Q and R), while concomitantly upregulating LAMP2 (a critical mediator of lysosomal lipid clearance) and PPARα (a key regulator of mitochondrial β-oxidation) proteins (Fig. 7Q–S, T). These physiologically beneficial effects were completely neutralized by IRS1 overexpression. Similarly, immunofluorescence staining of PLIN2 and BODIPY confirmed a marked decrease in lipid droplet accumulation following miR-487b-3p treatment, with IRS1 overexpression reversing this protective effect (Fig. 7U, Fig. S10A–B, Supporting Information).

Furthermore, our studies revealed that NCE-derived miR-487b-3p can effectively inhibit the pathological EndoMT process in SCMECs, as evidenced by significantly reduced α-SMA staining intensity (Fig. 7V, Fig. S10C, Supporting Information), which correlated with the observed reduction in lipid deposition. IRS1 overexpression restored the original mesenchymal phenotypic characteristics.

Collectively, these findings establish that NCE-derived miR-487b-3p exerts its protective effects through precise targeting and regulation of the IRS1-PI3K-Akt-mTOR signaling cascade. This sophisticated molecular intervention effectively suppresses pathological lipid biosynthesis in SCMECs, enhances cellular lipid catabolic capacity through coordinated regulation of lysosomal and mitochondrial pathways, and maintains endothelial phenotypic stability-all of which are critically important for preserving lipid metabolic homeostasis following SCI. The identified regulatory axis provides novel therapeutic targets for mitigating secondary damage in SCI pathophysiology and offers potential insights into the molecular mechanisms underlying neurovascular remodeling.

sec.title: miR-487b-3p inhibits myelin debris-induced Vasculo-immune CXCL12-CXCR4 signaling axis after SCI to attenuate the inflammatory cascade
sec.p: As a prioritized miR-487b-3p target, Cxcl2 and its secreted ligand CXCL12 represent pivotal regulators in SCI pathogenesis. While canonical studies delineate CXCL12's dichotomous role - exhibiting pro-regenerative effects at physiologic concentrations while paradoxically impairing axonal regeneration when sequestered within neurotrauma zones-our focus elucidates its maladaptive consequences [[44], [45], [46], [47]]. The CXCL12-CXCR4 chemokine axis, through paracrine signaling, orchestrates the spatiotemporal recruitment of CXCR4+ myeloid cells that establish immunosuppressive niches and promote pathologic fibrogenesis [48]. Notably, aberrant activation of this axis in the post-injury bone marrow niche contributes to acquired hematopoietic failure, creating a permissive environment for chronic neuroinflammation [[49], [50], [51], [52]]. Targeted modulation of this pathway emerges as a promising therapeutic strategy for SCI.

Our integrative bioinformatics analysis (GSE162610/GSE196928) revealed endothelial cell-restricted expression of CXCL12 (Fig. 8A). This temporal pattern was validated through quantitative Western blot, demonstrating progressive CXCL12 accumulation in spinal cord parenchyma (Fig. 8B and C) and myelin debris-stimulated SCMECs (Fig. S17A and B, Supporting Information), establishing a robust injury-responsive expression signature.Fig. 8
miR-487b-3p targeted regulation of the CXCL12-CXCR4 axis ameliorates the positive feedback loop between SCMECs and inflammatory-associated macrophages post-SCI. A) UMAP visualization of single-cell transcriptomic profiles showing the expression of Cxc12 enriched in endothelial cells. B, C) Representative Western blot and densitometric analysis (n = 3) of level of CXCL12 in spinal cord after SCI. D) Cell-type-specific expression of CXCR4 in the spinal cord, analyzed by single-cell RNA sequencing. E, F) Flow cytometry analysis of CXCR4+ macrophages (CD11b+F4/80+) in spinal cord tissue at 7 days post SCI of Sham, Control, ACE and NCE groups (n = 6). G) iTALK-based ligand-receptor interaction network between CXCR4+ macrophages and SCMECs at different time points post-SCI. H, I) Representative Western blot and densitometric analysis (n = 3) of level of GDF15 in CXCR4+ and CXCR4- macrophages. J, K) Representative Western blot and densitometric analysis (n = 3) of level of GDF15 in CXCR4+ macrophages transfected with si-GDF15 and si-NC. L) Schematic diagram of the co-culture of CXCR4+ and CXCR4- macrophages with SCMECs. M, N) Representative Western blot and densitometric analysis (n = 3) of level of Smad2/3 and p-Smad2/3 in SCMECs co-cultured with CXCR4+ macrophages, CXCR4- macrophages and CXCR4+ macrophages + si-GDF15. O, P) Representative immunofluorescence staining images of α-SMA (red), CD31 (green) and DAPI (blue) of SCMECs co-cultured with CXCR4+ macrophages, CXCR4- macrophages and CXCR4+ macrophages + si-GDF15. Q) Bubble chart showing the functional enrichment of genes upregulated in CXCR4+ macrophages relative to CXCR4- macrophages, based on single-cell RNA-seq data. R, S) Representative immunofluorescence staining images and quantitative analysis of length of TUBB3+ axons in PBS, CXCR4- macrophages and CXCR4+ macrophages groups (n = 6). T-U) Representative Western blot and densitometric analysis (n = 3) of level of α-syn and MBP in neurons co-cultured with PBS, CXCR4+ macrophages and CXCR4- macrophages. Data are presented as mean ± s.d. Statistical analysis was performed using one-way analysis of variance (ANOVA) for comparisons. ∗, p < 0.05; ∗∗, p < 0.01; ∗∗∗, p < 0.001; ∗∗∗∗, p < 0.0001; ns, no significance.

Functional interrogation confirmed miR-487b-3p′s direct regulatory role: mimic transfection induced significant Cxcl12 mRNA attenuation (Fig. 7C) and corresponding protein suppression (Fig. 7F and G). Mechanistically, mutagenesis of the predicted 3′UTR binding site (Cxcl12-3′UTR-mut) abrogated miR-487b-3p-mediated repression in dual-luciferase assays (Fig. 7H and I), pinpointing a canonical seed sequence interaction that governs this post-transcriptional control. To investigate the relationship between IRS1 and CXCL12, we established IRS1-overexpressing SCMECs. Western blot analysis showed that CXCL12 expression was correspondingly increased, while treatment with the miR-487b-3p mimic suppressed this upregulation. These results indicate that miR-487b-3p reduces CXCL12 secretion through at least two distinct mechanisms (Fig. S18A and B, Supporting Information).

Single-cell sequencing analysis revealed that CXCR4 was predominantly expressed in macrophages, indicating that macrophages are the primary cell type responsive to CXCL12 signaling (Fig. 8D). Temporal flow cytometric analysis conducted on day 7 post-injury demonstrated that CXCR4+ macrophage infiltration peaked (Fig. 8E and F), which coincided with the maximum CXCL12 concentration gradient in the lesion area. Transwell chemotaxis assays revealed that treatment with NCE or miR-487b-3p mimic significantly inhibited myelin debris-stimulated SCMEC-mediated macrophage migration (Figs. S15A, B, Figure S17C, D, E, Supporting Information). Furthermore, flow cytometry results indicated that in vivo administration of NCE@GelNB markedly decreased the number of CXCR4+ macrophages (Fig. 8E and F), whereas ACE treatment exerted no significant impact. These findings suggest that NCE and its encapsulated miR-487b-3p can significantly suppress the macrophage-recruiting chemokines secreted by SCMECs in response to myelin debris stimulation, and attenuate their responsiveness to this chemotactic signal.

iTALK ligand-receptor mapping uncovered evolving GDF15-TGFβR2 interactions between CXCR4+ macrophages and SCMECs (Fig. 8G). Elevated GDF15 expression in pro-inflammatory macrophages (Fig. 8H and I) was functionally validated through siRNA-mediated knockdown, which attenuated p-Smad2/3 activation (Fig. 8J–N) and prevented SCMEC mesenchymal transition (Fig. 8O and P), defining a non-canonical TGFβ pathway in injury-induced endothelial-mesenchymal transition.

To investigate the differences between CXCR4+ and CXCR4- macrophages. Firstly, based on conventional macrophage classification, immunofluorescence staining was performed on flow-sorted F4/80+CXCR4+ and F4/80+CXCR4- macrophages. The results demonstrated that the M1 marker CD86 exhibited stronger fluorescence intensity in F4/80+CXCR4+ macrophages, while the M2 marker CD206 was more highly expressed in F4/80+CXCR4- macrophages (Figs. S19A, B, C, Supporting Information). ScRNA-seq analysis was performed, which revealed excessive activation of the TNF-α/NF-κB signaling pathway (Fig. 8Q). Notably, this activation was closely associated with enhanced neurotoxic potential. To further validate this neurotoxicity, different types of macrophages were co-cultured with neuronal cells. Immunofluorescence staining demonstrated that CXCR4+ macrophages significantly impaired neuronal axon outgrowth (Fig. 8R and S). Consistently, Western blot analysis showed that CXCR4+ macrophages induced damage to synapses (α-synuclein, α-syn) and myelin (myelin basic protein, MBP) in primary neurons (Fig. 8T, U, V), collectively validating their pathological role in neurodegenerative processes.

These findings demonstrate that post-SCI, myelin debris-activated SCMECs secrete increased CXCL12, recruiting CXCR4+ macrophages to the lesion center. The elevated GDF15 secretion by CXCR4+ macrophages then acts on TGFβR2 receptor of SCMECs, exacerbating SCMECs mesenchymal transition and establishing a positive feedback cascade that promotes SCI progression. Notably, miR-487b-3p in NCE specifically targets the predicted Cxcl12 binding site, preventing CXCL12 protein formation and disrupting this microvascular-immune system positive feedback interaction (Scheme 1).Scheme 1
A) Comparison of lipid deposition and vascular integrity in microvessels and SCMECs at early and late stages after spinal cord injury in 2-day-old and 8-week-old mice. B) Isolation, extraction, and identification of NCE and ACE, as well as detection of their active components. C) Preparation and characterization of the “Microenvironment-Reprogramming Potent Hydrogel”. D) Schematic diagram illustrating that NCE delivers miR-487b-3p to microvessels at the SCI lesion site, thereby inhibiting the hyperactivation of IRS1–PI3K–Akt–mTOR signaling in SCMECs triggered by myelin debris. Excessive lipid overload leads to lysosomal dysfunction, which further induces the initiation and progression of EndoMT in SCMECs, accompanied by increased CXCL12 secretion that recruits CXCR4+ macrophages into the injured region. These macrophages secrete GDF15 and TNF-α, ultimately exacerbating secondary injury.

sec.title: Ago-miR-487b-3p@GelNB significantly promotes SCI repair
sec.p: To elucidate the in vivo reparative potential of miR-487b-3p encapsulated within NCE, we bioengineered Ago-miR and Ago-miR-NC constructs based on the precise miR-487b-3p sequence. These therapeutic moieties were incorporated into a biocompatible GelNB hydrogel matrix for targeted SCI intervention and subsequent comprehensive evaluation.

The hindlimb locomotor kinematics of mice in distinct treatment cohorts were meticulously recorded and digitally reconstructed over a 28-day post-treatment period (Fig. 9A–C). Quantitative gait analysis of the reconstructed trajectories, coupled with rigorous statistical assessment, demonstrated that Ago-miR-treated SCI mice exhibited significant motor function recovery. Notably, parameters including maximum iliac crest elevation, stride length, and maximal toe elevation were substantially ameliorated relative to both the control group and the Ago-miR-NC-treated cohort (Fig. 9D–F). Complementary MEP electrophysiology revealed that Ago-miR administration markedly shortened central conduction latency while simultaneously augmenting neural signal amplitude (Fig. 9G–I). These integrated behavioral and neurophysiological datasets provide compelling evidence that Ago-miR therapy confers substantial functional restoration in SCI-affected mice.Fig. 9
Ago-miR-487b-3p remodels lipid metabolic homeostasis in SCMECs to enhance functional recovery of SCI. A-F) Analysis of hindlimb locomotor function in Control, Ago-miR-487b-3p, and Ago-miR-NC groups following SCI, based on reconstructed hindlimb movement trajectories (A-C). Quantitative parameters (n = 6) including maximal iliac crest height (D), stride length (E), and maximal toe height (F) were measured to assess motor recovery. Data are shown for each experimental group. G-I) Detection of motor evoked potentials and quantitative statistical analysis of amplitude and latency in Control, Ago-miR-487b-3p, and Ago-miR-NC groups following SCI (n = 6). J-L) Immunofluorescence staining of GFAP (purple), Laminin (red) and CSPG (green) in the lesion center of SCI 28dpi in Control, Ago-miR-487b-3p, and Ago-miR-NC groups following SCI. Representative images and quantification results (n = 6) are shown. M, N) Representative images of AAV2/9-SYN-ZsGreen-labeled cortical projections (white) of each group (n = 6). Data are presented as mean ± s.d. Statistical analysis was performed using one-way analysis of variance (ANOVA) for comparisons. ∗, p < 0.05; ∗∗, p < 0.01; ∗∗∗, p < 0.001; ∗∗∗∗, p < 0.0001; ns, no significance.

Subsequent histopathological analysis focused on the spatiotemporal evolution of scar formation within the injury epicenter. ELISA detection revealed that treatment with NCE and Ago-miR did not significantly reduce the levels of GDF15 and TNF-α on day 3 of initial injury. However, at the peak of inflammatory cell infiltration on days 7 and 14, we observed a significant decrease in the levels of GDF15 and TNF-α following treatment with NCE and TNF-α. (Fig. S20A and B, Supporting Information). Immunofluorescence microscopy at the 28-day treatment endpoint (Fig. 9J–L) revealed that Ago-miR treatment elicited a profound reduction in the deposition of CSPG and Laminin within the lesional core, indicative of enhanced scar-free repair capacity. Advanced immunofluorescence interrogation further demonstrated that Ago-miR therapy significantly diminished intracellular lipid droplet accumulation, as evidenced by reduced PLIN2 staining in both microvascular structures and SCMECs (Fig. S21A and B, Supporting Information), thereby confirming its capacity to maintain endothelial lipid homeostasis in vivo.

Concomitantly, we observed marked inhibition of microvascular mesenchymal transition (Fig. S21C and F, Supporting Information) and attenuated secretion of pro-fibrotic extracellular matrix components, including collagen I and fibronectin, at the vascular periphery (Figs. S21D, G, E, H, Supporting Information). These integrated findings substantiate the pivotal role of miR-487b-3p in orchestrating a multifaceted reparative response through modulation of endothelial metabolic integrity and fibrogenic signaling.

Flow cytometric quantification of single cell suspensions prepared from 7-day post-injury spinal cord specimens (Fig. S21I and J, Supporting Information) revealed a significant attenuation of CXCR4+ macrophages infiltration within the injury penumbra following Ago-miR treatment, contrasting with untreated controls. This immunomodulatory effect establishes a permissive microenvironment for subsequent neuroregenerative processes.

Axonal tracing studies utilizing AAV2/9-SYN-ZsGreen-labeled corticospinal projections demonstrated robust axonal elongation across the lesion boundary into the caudal spinal cord segments at 28 day post-Ago-miR therapy (Fig. 9M and N), providing direct morphological evidence of enhanced neural circuit reconstruction.

In general, these data indicate that the scar-free repair in neonatal mice is partly attributed to the sEVs in the circulating plasma of neonatal mice, which reshape the lipid metabolic homeostasis of SCMECs. The miR-487b-3p contained in these sEVs targets and inhibits the expression of IRS1, reduces the conversion of myelin debris into lipid droplets by SCMECs, prevents excessive intracellular lipid deposition, inhibits the EndoMT process, and decreases the production of CXCL12, thereby improving the recruitment and infiltration of CXCR4+ macrophages. Additionally, the processes of TNF-α-mediated nerve axon damage and GDF15-exacerbated EndoMT and scar component secretion are also attenuated (Scheme 1).

This study not only uncovers a novel pathological pathway whereby scar formation impedes axonal regeneration following SCI but also novelly delineates the underlying regulatory mechanism of circulating plasma-derived sEVs from 2-day-old neonatal mice - with the microvasculature as the core - via modulation of endothelium-immune crosstalk, thereby furnishing a novel therapeutic strategy for SCI repair.

sec.title: Discussion
sec.p: Spinal cord injury represents one of the most intractable neuropathological conditions in neurology, characterized by irreversible functional deficits and a multifaceted pathogenesis involving neurodegenerative cascades, neuroinflammatory responses, and metabolic dysregulation [[53], [54], [55]]. Conventional therapeutic modalities, including stem cell transplantation and anti-inflammatory pharmacotherapies, have demonstrated limited efficacy due to suboptimal engraftment outcomes, allogeneic immune rejection, and off-target pharmacological effects [[56], [57], [58], [59]]. This investigation elucidates a previously uncharacterized mechanism whereby NCE facilitate SCI repair through age-specific metabolic reprogramming of SCMECs via the miR-487b-3p-IRS1/CXCL12 regulatory axis. This paradigm-shifting discovery not only addresses a critical knowledge gap concerning developmental “metabolic memory” utilization in extracellular vesicle-mediated tissue regeneration but also introduces a highly promising metabolic modulation strategy for SCI therapeutics.

Comparative analysis with ACE reveals that NCE possess distinctive regenerative advantages, underscoring the unique regenerative potency of “juvenile” biological factors. Neonatal tissues exhibit specialized metabolic phenotypes-notably enhanced glycolytic flux and oxidative phosphorylation efficiency-which facilitate rapid anabolic processes and tissue repair. Concordantly, NCE-mediated lipid droplet staining analyses demonstrate significant attenuation of lipid accumulation in damaged SCMECs, coupled with restoration of lipogenesis/lipolysis homeostasis, whereas ACE fail to induce comparable metabolic modulation. These findings indicate that NCE harbor a neonatal-specific “metabolic imprint” characterized by optimized lipid utilization kinetics and attenuated pro-inflammatory lipid signaling, effectively counteracting SCI-associated metabolic perturbations (e.g., lipotoxicity, endoplasmic reticulum stress). Notably, integrative behavioral and electrophysiological assessments directly correlate these metabolic improvements with functional recovery, aligning with emerging evidence that metabolic reprogramming of neural cells is pivotal for axonal regeneration and neurological functional restoration. Through targeted investigation of SCMECs-an understudied cellular component in SCI pathobiology-we have identified a previously unrecognized vascular-neural metabolic axis, thereby expanding the therapeutic landscape beyond conventional neuronal and astrocytic targets.

While the neuropathological community has increasingly acknowledged that lipid homeostasis disruption exacerbates tissue damage and documented the pathological role of early myelin debris in SCMECs activation, the intricate lipid metabolism mechanisms in SCMECs remain substantially underexplored [27,28,60]. This study demonstrates that endothelial lipid accumulation promotes a pathogenic triad of neuroinflammatory activation, vascular hyperpermeability, and fibrogenesis-the three cardinal processes underlying SCI scar formation. Pharmacological inhibition of lipid overload effectively abrogates SCMECs hyperactivation (e.g., downregulation of VCAM-1 expression and subsequent macrophage recruitment), thereby disrupting the self-perpetuating “lipid accumulation-inflammation-fibrosis” pathogenic circuit. This dual-modulatory effect on metabolic and inflammatory pathways highlights the pleiotropic therapeutic potential of targeting SCMECs lipid metabolism for comprehensive SCI management.

This investigation identifies the IRS1/CXCL12 signaling nexus as a master regulator of NCE - induced metabolic reprogramming. CXCL12, a well-established chemotactic cytokine for stem cell and immune cell recruitment [[61], [62], [63], [64]], exhibits upregulated expression in injured SCMECs, while NCE administration potently suppresses its expression. Mechanistically, we demonstrate that NCE exerts this regulatory effect through targeted inhibition of the IRS1/PI3K/Akt transcriptional regulatory pathway, which directly governs CXCL12 gene expression. This finding is congruent with previous studies showing that IRS1/PI3K/Akt pathway activation drives both physiological and pathological CXCL12 expression in endothelial cells, contributing to aberrant angiogenesis and neuroinflammatory processes in damaged tissues. The IRS1/PI3K/Akt pathway exhibits context - dependent dual functions in physiological and pathological processes. Under physiological conditions, transient and moderate activation maintains cellular homeostasis, metabolism, survival, angiogenesis, and tissue repair. In contrast, aberrant, sustained, or excessive activation disrupts metabolic balance, suppresses autophagic flux, promotes lipid accumulation, and drives pathological states such as inflammation, fibrosis, and maladaptive cell remodeling. The biological outcome of this pathway is largely determined by the magnitude, duration, and spatiotemporal dynamics of its activation. By disrupting the IRS1-CXCL12 molecular interface, NCE reconstructs the local metabolic-immune signaling microenvironment, thereby establishing a pro-regenerative niche. This discovery not only elucidates the molecular basis of NCE - mediated fibrosis attenuation and anti-inflammatory effects but also positions the IRS1/CXCL12 axis as a potential therapeutic target for vascular metabolic dysfunction-associated pathologies, including atherosclerotic vascular disease and diabetic retinopathy.

miR-487b-3p is identified as the principal effector molecule within NCE, providing a critical mechanistic link for its therapeutic effects. Functional studies using Ago-miR-487b-3p mimics successfully recapitulate the metabolic and functional improvements induced by NCE, thereby confirming its central regulatory role. Molecular characterization reveals that miR-487b-3p directly targets IRS1, a canonical insulin signaling adaptor protein, thereby explaining its inhibitory effects on the IRS1/PI3K/Akt/CXCL12 signaling cascade. This finding is particularly significant given emerging evidence that extracellular vesicle-associated miRNAs can function as “metabolic signaling mediators” coordinating intercellular metabolic communication. For instance, tissue-specific miRNAs such as liver-enriched miR-122 [65] and muscle-specific miR-133a [66] regulate systemic metabolic homeostasis through sEVs-mediated delivery, suggesting that developmental stage-specific miRNAs may encode “tissue-specific metabolic instructions.” In the context of SCI, NCE - derived miR-487b-3p serves as a “metabolic reprogramming factor” that restores SCMEC lipid metabolism-a function that is attenuated in adult sEVs, potentially contributing to age-associated declines in endogenous regenerative capacity. Besides SCMECs, astrocytes, microglia, neurons, and oligodendrocytes also play critical roles in spinal cord injury repair. The potential functions of miR-487b-3p in these cell types warrant further investigation.

Beyond the pivotal role of miR-487b-3p identified in this study, several other highly abundant miRNAs within NCE, including miR-665-3p and miR-380-3p, may also contribute to the overall therapeutic outcomes in spinal cord injury repair. Based on bioinformatic predictions and previous reports, these co-enriched miRNAs are potentially involved in the regulation of lipid metabolism, inflammatory activation, endothelial-mesenchymal transition, and vascular-immune crosstalk, all of which are closely linked to secondary injury progression and microenvironmental remodeling. It is reasonable to infer that these miRNAs may function synergistically with miR-487b-3p rather than act independently, possibly by targeting overlapping or complementary signaling networks that converge on the IRS1/CXCL12 axis or parallel pathways governing lipid deposition, macrophage polarization, and vascular homeostasis. Such cooperative effects could further enhance the clearance of myelin debris, restore lysosomal function, and improve axonal integrity. Nevertheless, the specific downstream targets, regulatory hierarchies, and precise synergistic mechanisms among these miRNAs remain to be systematically elucidated in future investigations. The current study focused on establishing the functional core of miR-487b-3p, which serves as a key mediator underlying the beneficial effects of NCE in SCI repair.

Cell-free therapy mediated by small extracellular vesicles represents a promising strategy for spinal cord injury repair, but its therapeutic efficacy largely depends on suitable delivery vehicles and efficient local retention. Although conventional GelMA has been widely applied as a carrier for exosome delivery, it exhibits several inherent drawbacks, including insufficient structural stability, uncontrolled degradation, and limited tissue adhesion, which hinder sustained vesicle release and stable anchoring at the injury site. Furthermore, traditional delivery systems lack intrinsic targeting capacity, thereby restricting efficient enrichment of exosomes at the injured vasculature and limiting their translational potential [67,68]. Despite these promising therapeutic outcomes in the mouse model, several important challenges must be addressed for the clinical translation of neonatal blood-derived sEV products and the “Microenvironment-Reprogramming Potent Hydrogel”. First, potential immunogenicity of allogeneic or xenogeneic neonatal biological materials warrants careful evaluation to avoid adverse immune reactions in future clinical applications. Second, scalable and standardized isolation and purification of sEVs from blood components remains technically demanding, requiring optimized manufacturing processes to ensure consistent yield, purity, and bioactivity. Third, ethical approval, informed consent, and standardized sourcing of neonatal blood components represent critical logistical and regulatory considerations that must be strictly followed in accordance with institutional and national guidelines. Future studies will focus on engineering synthetic or iPSC-derived sEVs that recapitulate the juvenile metabolic and regenerative signatures, thereby circumventing ethical constraints and improving clinical feasibility while retaining the core therapeutic efficacy observed in this study.

In contrast, the “Microenvironment-Reprogramming Potent Hydrogel” constructed in this study - GelNB hydrogel loaded with NCE - achieves a comprehensive breakthrough over traditional carriers through synergistic structural and functional optimization. GelNB not only possesses a porous structure with high porosity and uniform interconnectivity but also exhibits excellent mechanical performance that can adapt to the mechanical stress fluctuations in the injured area after SCI. Its strong tissue adhesiveness ensures stable retention of the hydrogel at the injury site, providing a long-acting and homogeneous sustained-release microenvironment for NCE. Mechanistically, this composite hydrogel precisely regulates lipid metabolic homeostasis in SCMECs, reducing the interference of abnormal lipid deposition on endothelial phenotypic transition, thereby effectively inhibiting their EndoMT process and maintaining the integrity of the spinal microvascular barrier. Meanwhile, bioactive components in NCE can specifically downregulate the expression of CXCR4 chemokine in the injured area, significantly reducing the recruitment and infiltration of CXCR4+ macrophages, alleviating oxidative stress damage induced by excessive inflammatory responses, and protecting neural axons from fragmentation and degeneration.

This multi-target regulatory mode innovatively integrates the microenvironmental adaptability of the carrier with the bioactivity of exosomes, distinguishing itself from existing studies that solely focus on exosome bioactivity or single carrier performance. It achieves a “three-in-one” therapeutic effect of “microenvironment reprogramming - bioactive factor delivery - pathological process inhibition”. This strategy not only provides a more efficient and stable therapeutic regimen for SCI repair but also offers a new paradigm for the synergistic design of carriers and bioactive components in cell-free therapy. Its injectability and biosafety are more compatible with the requirements of clinical minimally invasive surgery, laying a solid foundation for subsequent clinical translation studies.

sec.title: Conclusion
sec.p: Based on the study of scarless repair in 2-day-old neonatal mice with SCI, this research is the first to identify that NCE serve as key mediators promoting lipid metabolic reprogramming in SCMECs. These NCE alleviate lipid overload and the occurrence of EndoMT, reduce the recruitment and infiltration of pro-inflammatory and pro-fibrotic inflammatory cells, and effectively attenuate the “inflammatory storm” following SCI. Notably, as blood-borne exosomes, NCE naturally exhibit excellent inherent vascular targeting capacity. More interestingly, the highly abundant factor miR-487b-3p identified by sequencing has unveiled a novel pathophysiological mechanism post-SCI: early-phase SCMECs that phagocytose myelin debris display “excessive self-protection,” leading to hyperactivation of the PI3K-Akt signaling pathway. This hyperactivation results in excessive lipid deposition that exceeds lysosomal processing capacity, thereby inducing mesenchymal transition of SCMECs. In turn, mesenchymal transitioned SCMECs secrete increased levels of CXCL12, which recruits more CXCR4+ macrophages from the periphery to the injured core area, further exacerbating tissue damage. Beyond secreting GDF15 to promote EndoMT, CXCR4+ macrophages also release inflammatory factors such as TNF-α that further damage neural axons, generating more myelin debris. This forms a closed-loop positive feedback destructive cascade, ultimately leading to scar formation in the SCI lesion area and rendering SCI treatment extremely challenging. Importantly, miR-487b-3p specifically targets and inhibits the upstream gene IRS1 of the PI3K-Akt signaling pathway as well as CXCL12 secreted post-mesenchymal transition, which highlights the potential therapeutic value of NCE from neonatal circulating blood for SCI. Finally, to overcome the low adhesion defect of conventional hydrogels, we first introduced the newly developed high-adhesion material GelNB into SCI therapy. By homogenizing NCE into GelNB, we developed a bioactive novel material termed “Microenvironment-Reprogramming Potent Hydrogel,” which opens up new avenues for the mechanistic investigation and treatment of spinal cord injury.

sec.title: Experimental section
sec.p: Detailed methods are provided in the Supporting Information.

sec.title: Ethics approval and consent to participate
sec.p: All animal experiments were conducted in accordance with the guidelines for the care and use of laboratory animals. The study was approved by the Laboratory Animal Ethics Committee of Xiangya Hospital, Central South University (approval number: Animal Ethics Review (2025)03688). All procedures followed the 3R principles. This study does not involve human participants, so informed consent is not applicable.

sec.title: Background
sec.p: Spinal cord injury (SCI) is a devastating condition with high disability rates. In the secondary injury phase, activated microglia play a central role by mediating the inflammatory response. Notably, pro-inflammatory forms of programmed cell death, including PANoptosis, can exacerbate inflammatory cascade. Although microglia-driven inflammation is well-characterized in SCI, it remains unclear whether microglia undergo PANoptosis. Furthermore, potential therapeutic agents targeting this specific process remain to be discovered in SCI.

sec.title: Methods
sec.p: We performed an integrated analysis of bulk RNA-seq and scRNA-seq to investigate the heterogeneity of programmed cell death and PANoptosis across different cell types. Candidate genes associated with high PANoptosis activity scores were identified using correlation analysis. Furthermore, four machine learning algorithms were applied to screen for core genes. By combining transcription factor (TF) activity prediction and Chip-atlas database analysis, we identified TFs that potentially regulate PANoptosis. Validation experiments were conducted at each analytical stage using animal and cellular models.

sec.title: Results
sec.p: Pyroptosis, apoptosis, and necroptosis, were persistently upregulated following SCI. Notably, the PANoptosis pathway was significantly enriched and predominantly localized to microglia. Further investigation identified ZBP1 as prominently upregulated PANoptosome sensor associated with microglial PANoptosis. Moreover, IRF1 was found to be enriched at the promoter region of Zbp1 and transcriptionally regulated its expression. Pharmacological inhibition of IRF1 was accompanied by reduced ZBP1 expression, suppression of PANoptosis-related execution markers, decreased TNF-α and IL-6 release, and attenuation of M1-like microglial polarization.

sec.title: Conclusion
sec.p: These findings highlight the IRF1–ZBP1 axis as a regulatory mechanism of PANoptosis in microglia following SCI and suggest it as a potential therapeutic target to modulate neuroinflammation.
